title: 2025 Session 1 exercises
markdown:
## Session 1: Exchange Rates & Forward Markets — Exercises

---

### Question 1.1 — Euro/Dollar Appreciation & Depreciation

If the exchange rate value of the euro goes from US\$1.15 to US\$1.05, then:

- a) The euro has appreciated, and Europeans will find U.S. goods cheaper.
- b) The euro has appreciated, and Europeans will find U.S. goods more expensive.
- c) **The euro has depreciated, and Europeans will find U.S. goods more expensive.**
- d) The euro has depreciated, and Europeans will find U.S. goods cheaper.

> **Key concept:** When the euro buys fewer dollars (1.15 → 1.05), the euro has **depreciated**. Europeans now need more euros to purchase the same dollar-priced goods, making U.S. goods **more expensive** for them.

---

### Question 1.2 — Korean Won / U.S. Dollar

The won/dollar exchange rate has moved from **800 KRW/USD** to **1,000 KRW/USD**.

**a)** Has the Korean won appreciated or depreciated with respect to the dollar? By what percentage?

$$
\text{% change in won} = \frac{1/1000 - 1/800}{1/800} = \frac{0.001 - 0.00125}{0.00125} = -20\%
$$

> The Korean won has **depreciated by 20%** against the dollar (it now takes more won to buy one dollar).

**b)** By what percentage has the value of the dollar changed with respect to the won?

$$
\text{% change in dollar} = \frac{1000 - 800}{800} = +25\%
$$

> The dollar has **appreciated by 25%** relative to the won.

---

### Question 1.3 — Zimbabwe Dollar Devaluation

On August 8, 2000, Zimbabwe changed the value of the Zim dollar from **Z\$38/US\$** to **Z\$50/US\$**.

**a)** U.S. dollar value of the Zim dollar:

$$
\text{Original: } \frac{1}{38} \approx \text{US\$}0.02632 \quad;\quad \text{New: } \frac{1}{50} = \text{US\$}0.02000
$$

**b)** Percent devaluation of the Zim dollar relative to the U.S. dollar:

$$
\frac{0.02000 - 0.02632}{0.02632} = \frac{-0.00632}{0.02632} \approx -24.0\%
$$

> The Zim dollar was **devalued by approximately 24%**.

**c)** Percent appreciation of the U.S. dollar relative to the Zim dollar:

$$
\frac{50 - 38}{38} = \frac{12}{38} \approx +31.6\%
$$

> The U.S. dollar **appreciated by approximately 31.6%** against the Zim dollar.

---

### Question 1.4 — Forward Exchange Rates (Covered Interest Rate Parity)

**Given:**
- Spot rate: $S = 1.1795$ USD/EUR
- 1-year rates: $r_{EUR} = 4\%$, $r_{USD} = 5\%$
- 1-month rates: $r_{EUR} = 3\%$, $r_{USD} = 4\%$

> **Covered Interest Rate Parity (CIP):**
> $$F = S \times \frac{1 + r_{USD} \times \frac{n}{12}}{1 + r_{EUR} \times \frac{n}{12}}$$

**a)** One-year forward exchange rate ($n = 12$):

$$
F_{1y} = 1.1795 \times \frac{1 + 0.05}{1 + 0.04} = 1.1795 \times \frac{1.05}{1.04} \approx 1.1908 \;\text{USD/EUR}
$$

**b)** One-month forward exchange rate ($n = 1$):

$$
F_{1m} = 1.1795 \times \frac{1 + 0.04 \times \frac{1}{12}}{1 + 0.03 \times \frac{1}{12}} = 1.1795 \times \frac{1.003333}{1.002500} \approx 1.1805 \;\text{USD/EUR}
$$

---

### Question 1.5 — Swedish Krona Forward Rate & Premium

**Given:**
- Spot rate: $S = 5.7$ SKr/USD
- 3-month annualized rates: $r_{SKr} = 12\%$, $r_{USD} = 8\%$

**a)** Three-month forward exchange rate:

$$
F_{3m} = S \times \frac{1 + r_{SKr} \times \frac{3}{12}}{1 + r_{USD} \times \frac{3}{12}} = 5.7 \times \frac{1 + 0.03}{1 + 0.02} = 5.7 \times \frac{1.03}{1.02} \approx 5.7559 \;\text{SKr/USD}
$$

**b)** Annualized forward premium (or discount) on the dollar relative to the krona:

$$
\text{Premium} = \frac{F - S}{S} \times \frac{12}{n} = \frac{5.7559 - 5.7}{5.7} \times \frac{12}{3} \approx 0.98\% \times 4 \approx 3.92\%
$$

> The dollar is at an **annualized premium of approximately 3.92%** relative to the krona (equivalently, the krona is at a discount against the dollar). This reflects the higher Swedish interest rate.

---

### Question 1.6 — Covered Interest Arbitrage

**Given:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Spot rate | USD 1.46/EUR |
| 1-year forward rate | USD 1.49/EUR |
| Interest rate (EUR) | 7% annualized |
| Interest rate (USD) | 9% annualized |

**Step 1 — Check CIP equilibrium:**

$$
F^* = S \times \frac{1 + r_{USD}}{1 + r_{EUR}} = 1.46 \times \frac{1.09}{1.07} \approx 1.4873 \;\text{USD/EUR}
$$

The **theoretical forward rate** is $\approx 1.4873$, but the **quoted forward rate** is $1.49$.

Since $F_{quoted} > F^*$, the euro is **overpriced in the forward market** — an arbitrage opportunity exists.

**Step 2 — Arbitrage strategy (sell EUR forward):**

1. **Borrow** USD at 9%
2. **Convert** to EUR at the spot rate (1.46 USD/EUR)
3. **Invest** EUR at 7% for one year
4. **Sell** EUR forward at 1.49 USD/EUR (locking in the future exchange rate)

**Step 3 — Flows (per EUR 1 invested):**

- Borrow USD 1.46 → Convert to EUR 1.00 at spot
- After 1 year: EUR 1.00 × 1.07 = **EUR 1.07**
- Sell EUR 1.07 forward: 1.07 × 1.49 = **USD 1.5943**
- Repay USD loan: 1.46 × 1.09 = **USD 1.5914**

$$
\text{Arbitrage profit} = 1.5943 - 1.5914 = \text{USD } 0.0029 \text{ per EUR invested}
$$

> **Yes**, a covered arbitrage profit exists. The profit is small (≈ 0.20% per EUR) but risk-free, arising because the quoted forward rate deviates from the CIP-implied rate.
